sections.title: Relative Clauses
sections.explanation: Relative clauses provide additional information about a noun (the antecedent). They are introduced by a relative pronoun that agrees in GENDER and NUMBER with the antecedent, but takes the CASE required by its role in the relative clause. The relative pronouns are: Masc: der/den/dem/dessen, Fem: die/der/der/deren, Neut: das/das/dem/dessen, Plural: die/die/denen/deren. The verb in the relative clause goes to the END. The clause is set off by commas. Examples: 'Das Buch, das ich lese, ist spannend.' (The book that I'm reading is exciting.) — 'das' is neuter (Buch is neuter) and accusative (it's the object of 'lese'). 'Der Mann, der dort steht, ist mein Onkel.' — 'der' is masculine nominative (he's the subject of 'steht').
sections.tip: Two steps for relative pronouns: (1) Match the gender/number of the antecedent. (2) Choose the case based on the role in the relative clause (subject = nominative, object = accusative, etc.). The relative pronoun looks like the definite article, except: dative plural = 'denen' (not 'den'), and genitive adds '-en/-s' (dessen/deren).

examples.de: Das Buch, das ich lese, ist sehr interessant.
examples.en: The book that I'm reading is very interesting. (neut, acc)

examples.de: Der Mann, der dort sitzt, ist mein Lehrer.
examples.en: The man who is sitting there is my teacher. (masc, nom)

examples.de: Die Frau, der ich helfe, ist sehr nett.
examples.en: The woman I'm helping is very nice. (fem, dat — helfen takes dative)

examples.de: Die Kinder, mit denen wir spielen, sind unsere Nachbarn.
examples.en: The children we are playing with are our neighbors. (plural, dat)

examples.de: Das ist der Schauspieler, dessen Film ich gesehen habe.
examples.en: That is the actor whose film I saw. (masc, genitive)

exercises.prompt: Das Buch, ___ ich kaufen möchte, ist leider ausverkauft. (The book I want to buy is unfortunately sold out.)
exercises.explanation: 'Buch' is neuter (das Buch). In the relative clause, the book is the accusative object of 'kaufen'. Neuter accusative relative pronoun = 'das'.

exercises.prompt: Die Frau, ___ dort wohnt, ist meine Ärztin. (The woman who lives there is my doctor.)
exercises.hint: 'Frau' is feminine. In the relative clause, 'she' is the subject (nominative). Feminine nominative relative pronoun = ?

exercises.prompt: Der Student, ___ ich das Buch gegeben habe, ist sehr fleißig.
exercises.explanation: 'Student' is masculine. In the relative clause, the student is the indirect object of 'gegeben habe' (I gave him the book) = dative. Masculine dative relative pronoun = 'dem'.

exercises.prompt: Das Hotel, in ___ wir gewohnt haben, war wunderschön.
exercises.hint: 'Hotel' is neuter. After 'in' (location = dative), neuter dative relative pronoun = ?

exercises.prompt: Match each relative pronoun to its gender and case:
Die Kinder, ___ wir geholfen haben, waren sehr dankbar. (The children we helped were very grateful.)
exercises.hint: 'Kinder' is plural. 'helfen' takes dative. Plural dative relative pronoun = ?

exercises.prompt: Where does the verb go in a relative clause?
options: At the end of the clause
exercises.explanation: Relative clauses are subordinate clauses, so the verb goes to the end of the clause: 'Das Buch, das ich lese...' (lese at end).

exercises.prompt: Der Kollege, ___ Auto kaputt ist, kommt heute zu spät. (The colleague whose car is broken will be late today.)
exercises.hint: Genitive relative pronoun — masculine/neuter genitive. 'Das Auto gehört dem Kollegen' → possessive relationship.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence has the correct relative clause structure?
options: Das ist das Café, das ich mag sehr.
Das ist das Café, das ich sehr mag.
Das ist das Café, das sehr ich mag.
Das ist das Café, sehr das ich mag.
exercises.explanation: 'das ich sehr mag' — verb 'mag' at the end, adverb 'sehr' before the verb. All elements of the relative clause come before the final verb.

sections.title: Extended Noun Modifiers (Erweitertes Attribut)
sections.explanation: German allows a long attributive modifier to be placed before a noun, often replacing a relative clause. This is common in written/formal German. Structure: article + [participial phrase or adjective phrase] + noun. The participial phrase can include adverbs and prepositional phrases: 'der gestern angekommene Zug' (the train that arrived yesterday) — compare with the relative clause: 'der Zug, der gestern angekommen ist'. The past participle or present participle is placed just before the noun and takes adjective endings. Present participle (Partizip I) = infinitive + d: singen → singend, fahren → fahrend. These modify like adjectives: 'die singende Frau' (the singing woman), 'das schlafende Kind' (the sleeping child).
sections.tip: Extended noun modifiers are most common in newspapers, academic texts, and formal writing. Recognizing them is essential for reading advanced German. To decode them: find the noun, then look back to the article at the beginning — everything between is the modifier. Participial phrases use Partizip I (present, ongoing: fahrend) or Partizip II (past/completed: gebaut).

examples.en: the train that arrived yesterday (= der Zug, der gestern angekommen ist)
the sleeping child (Partizip I: schlafen → schlafend → schlafende)
a car manufactured in Germany (Partizip II)

examples.de: die von der Regierung beschlossenen Maßnahmen
examples.en: the measures decided by the government

examples.de: ein auf dem Markt erhältliches Produkt
examples.en: a product available on the market

exercises.prompt: What is the Partizip I (present participle) of 'schlafen'?
exercises.explanation: Partizip I = infinitive + -d: 'schlafen' → 'schlafend'. When used as an adjective (attributively), it takes endings: 'schlafendes Kind'.

exercises.prompt: Convert to extended noun modifier: 'das Kind, das singt' → 'das ___ Kind'
exercises.hint: Partizip I of 'singen' = singend. Add adjective ending for neuter nominative (das Kind): -e.

exercises.prompt: Decode the extended modifier: 'Der im letzten Jahr veröffentlichte Roman...'
options: The novel that was published a long time ago...
The novel that will be published next year...
The novel that was published last year...
The novel published by the author last year...
exercises.explanation: 'im letzten Jahr veröffentlichte' = published last year. 'veröffentlicht' is the Partizip II of 'veröffentlichen', used as an extended modifier before 'Roman'.

exercises.prompt: Convert to a relative clause: 'das in Berlin gebaute Haus' → 'das Haus, ___ in Berlin gebaut ___'
exercises.hint: Relative clause using passive: 'das Haus, das in Berlin gebaut wurde'

exercises.prompt: Which extended noun modifier is formed correctly? (spannend, das Buch — neuter)
exercises.explanation: 'das spannende Buch' — after a definite article (das), neuter nominative adjective ending = -e. 'spannend' is Partizip I of 'spannen', used as adjective meaning 'exciting'.

exercises.prompt: What is the Partizip I of 'wachsen' (to grow)?
Match each extended modifier to the relative clause it replaces:

sections.title: Infinitive Constructions with zu
sections.explanation: German frequently uses 'zu + infinitive' constructions instead of full subordinate clauses. The infinitive with 'zu' is used: (1) After many verbs: versuchen (to try), anfangen (to begin), aufhören (to stop), vergessen (to forget), vorhaben (to plan), etc. Example: 'Ich versuche, Deutsch zu lernen.' (2) After adjectives: Es ist wichtig, pünktlich zu sein. (3) As a subject or object clause replacing 'dass': 'Deutsch zu lernen ist schwer' = 'Es ist schwer, Deutsch zu lernen.' With separable verbs, 'zu' is inserted between prefix and verb: 'aufhören' → 'aufzuhören', 'einladen' → 'einzuladen'. Crucially: 'zu + infinitive' implies the SAME subject as the main clause — if the subject differs, use 'dass' instead.
sections.tip: The 'zu' goes directly before the infinitive (or between prefix and stem for separable verbs): 'zu lernen', 'aufzuhören', 'anzufangen'. No comma if the zu-infinitive phrase is short; a comma is used if the zu-phrase is extended or comes first. Same subject rule: 'Ich versuche, zu kommen' (I try to come — same subject) but 'Ich bitte ihn, dass er kommt' (I ask him to come — different subject).

examples.de: Er hat vergessen, die Tür abzuschließen.
examples.en: He forgot to lock the door. (separable: ab+zu+schließen)

examples.de: Es ist interessant, andere Kulturen kennenzulernen.
examples.en: It's interesting to get to know other cultures.

examples.de: Sie haben vor, nächsten Sommer nach Japan zu reisen.
examples.en: They plan to travel to Japan next summer.

examples.de: Ich hoffe, bald wieder gesund zu sein.
examples.en: I hope to be healthy again soon.

exercises.prompt: Ich versuche, mehr Sport ___ machen.
exercises.explanation: 'zu + infinitive' — 'zu machen' goes at the end of the infinitive phrase. 'Ich versuche, mehr Sport zu machen.'

exercises.prompt: Er hat vergessen, seine Hausaufgaben ___ (machen).
exercises.hint: 'zu + infinitive' after 'vergessen'. The infinitive goes to the end.

exercises.prompt: Where does 'zu' go with a separable verb like 'aufhören'?
options: It's not used with separable verbs.
exercises.explanation: With separable verbs, 'zu' is inserted between the prefix and the verb stem: auf+zu+hören = 'aufzuhören'. This stays as one word.

exercises.prompt: Es ist wichtig, regelmäßig ___ (schlafen) — zu-infinitive construction.
exercises.hint: 'zu + infinitive' after an adjective construction with 'Es ist + adj.'

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'zu + infinitive'?
options: Ich fange an, Gitarre zu lernen.
Ich fange zu an, Gitarre lernen.
Ich fange an Gitarre zu lernen.
exercises.explanation: 'Ich fange an, Gitarre zu lernen' — 'anfangen' takes 'zu + infinitive'. Comma before the extended infinitive phrase, 'zu' before the infinitive at the end.

exercises.prompt: Sie hat aufgehört, Fleisch ___ (essen). (She has stopped eating meat.)
exercises.hint: 'aufhören' + 'zu + infinitive'. 'essen' is a regular infinitive here.

exercises.prompt: Which construction should replace the 'zu-infinitive' because the subjects differ?
options: Ich bitte ihn, früher zu kommen.
Ich bitte ihn zu kommen früher.
Ich bitte ihn, dass er früher kommt.
Both A and C are correct.
exercises.explanation: Both are correct! When the subject of the dependent action is expressed by an accusative object ('ihn'), 'zu + infinitive' is possible AND standard: 'Ich bitte ihn, früher zu kommen' OR 'Ich bitte ihn, dass er früher kommt'. The zu-infinitive option is actually more common here.

exercises.prompt: Sie plant, im Sommer ___ (verreisen — separable: ver+reisen is NOT separable; use: 'wegfahren').
exercises.hint: For separable verb 'wegfahren': weg+zu+fahren = 'wegzufahren'. For 'verreisen' (inseparable): 'zu verreisen'.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its correct 'zu + infinitive' form:
